coredata.dc:title: Detection of α-fetoprotein with an ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence paper device based on green-luminescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lina
Li, Long
Ma, Chao
Ge, Shenguang
Yan, Mei
Bian, Cuirong
coredata.dc:description: A facile one-pot solvothermal method based on exfoliating and disintegrating treatments for graphite oxide was developed to prepare water-soluble graphene quantum dots. Dimethylformamide served as a solvent and source of nitrogen, the as-prepared nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) exhibited bright green emission under ultraviolet irradiation (∼365nm). More importantly, an ultrasensitive paper-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunoassay was developed with green-luminescent N-GQDs as ECL labels and α-fetoprotein antigen (AFP) as model protein. The N-GQDs were promising ECL labels because of their low anodic ECL potential of +0.84V and high biocompatibity, which can facilitate highly sensitive ECL bioassays. A novel paper working electrode was fabricated through a seed-mediated growth approach and served as a promising platform for antibodies attachment. Upon the immunorecognition of the immobilized AFP to its antibody labeled with N-GQDs, the proposed immunoassay displayed increasing ECL intensity, leading to a wide calibration range of 0.005–100ngmL−1 with a detection limit of 1.2pgmL−1 [signal-to-noise ratio (S/N)=3]. The ECL immunoassay for real samples displayed very similar results with the commercial ECL immunoassay, which provided a powerful avenue for the design of the ultrasensitive detection method for clinical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoflowers
Paper working electrode
Electrochemiluminescence immunoassay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the paper-based ECL immunosensor
The analytical performance of ECL immunosensor
Selectivity, reproducibility, stability of the ECL immunosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of ECL immunosensor preparation process.

(A) Illustration of the structural formula of N-GQDs; (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs aqueous solution; (C) fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-GQDs aqueous solution; (D) FT-IR spectra of GO and N-GQDs.
(A) TEM image of AuNPs seeds; (B) SEM images of cellulose paper; with the growth time of AuNPs increased, the SEM images of cellulose fibers in paper sample zone (C: 5min, D: 10min).

(A) CVs and (B) EIS of the PWE with different surface conditions in 10.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.5M KCl, bare PWE and Au-PWE; (C) ECL curves of the immunosensor in the absence (a) and presence (b) of DBAE by cycling the potential between 0 and 1.0V at 100mVs−1 using MWCNTs/N-GQDs/Ab2 labels, (c) using N-GQDs/Ab2 labels in the presence of DBAE.

(A) ECL intensity curves of the ECL immunosensor in the presence of different concentrations of AFP in PB containing 20.0mM DBAE; (B) calibration curves corresponding to the detection of AFP concentration over the full linear range (0.005, 0.01, .0.1, 1, 10, 50, 100ngmL−1); (C) specificity of the ECL immunosensor; (D) stability of the ECL immunosensor in PB containing 20.0mM DBAE with AFP at 1.0ngmL−1 level at a scan rate of 100mVs−1.
Analytical performance of various immunoassays for the detection of AFP.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: MPS, mesoporous silica; GO, graphite oxide; QDs, quantum dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Assay results of clinical serum samples using the proposed and reference methods.

head.ce:title: Detection of α-fetoprotein with an ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence paper device based on green-luminescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qilu Hospital of Shandong University, Jinan 250012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile one-pot solvothermal method based on exfoliating and disintegrating treatments for graphite oxide was developed to prepare water-soluble graphene quantum dots. Dimethylformamide served as a solvent and source of nitrogen, the as-prepared nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) exhibited bright green emission under ultraviolet irradiation (∼365nm). More importantly, an ultrasensitive paper-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunoassay was developed with green-luminescent N-GQDs as ECL labels and α-fetoprotein antigen (AFP) as model protein. The N-GQDs were promising ECL labels because of their low anodic ECL potential of +0.84V and high biocompatibity, which can facilitate highly sensitive ECL bioassays. A novel paper working electrode was fabricated through a seed-mediated growth approach and served as a promising platform for antibodies attachment. Upon the immunorecognition of the immobilized AFP to its antibody labeled with N-GQDs, the proposed immunoassay displayed increasing ECL intensity, leading to a wide calibration range of 0.005–100ngmL−1 with a detection limit of 1.2pgmL−1 [signal-to-noise ratio (S/N)=3]. The ECL immunoassay for real samples displayed very similar results with the commercial ECL immunoassay, which provided a powerful avenue for the design of the ultrasensitive detection method for clinical application.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of new type of quantum dots (QDs) with controllable properties provides opportunities for fabrication and design of new devices with extraordinary properties and functions [1]. Recently, fluorescent carbon based nanomaterials, including carbon dots (C-dots), graphene oxide nanoparticles, and the emerging graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are attracting increasing interest in many fields for their good biocompatibility and photostability [2–4]. GQDs have attracted more and more attention due to their special advantages especially, such as low toxicity, high fluorescent activity, robust chemical inertness, and excellent photostability. However, like carbon dots, the quantum yield is usually very low. Therefore, the preparation of GQDs with high quantum yield is demanding and important. To this end, many methods for modulating the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs and improving its quantum yield have been reported [5].

In view of the remarkable quantum-confinement and edge effects of GQDs, doping GQDs with heteroatoms could drastically alter their electronic characteristics and offer more active sites, thus producing new phenomena and unexpected properties [6]. The N atom, having a comparable atomic size and five valence electrons for bonding with carbon atoms, has been widely used for chemical doping of carbon nanomaterials. For instance, nitrogen doped carbon nanotubes showed highly effective electrocatalytic activities for the oxygen reduction reaction [7]. Similarly, doping of graphene with substituent N heteroatoms could effectively modulate the band gap of graphene to introduce new properties for device applications. For instance, Li et al. developed a simple yet effective electrochemical strategy for generating blue luminescence nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with O-rich functional groups, which show unique optoelectronic features distinctive from those of their N-free counterparts [1]. N-GQDs supported by graphene sheets have been demonstrated to possess superior electrocatalytic ability. Some QDs, such as CdTe [8], C-dots [9] and CdS [10], can be used as electrochemiluminescence (ECL) regents in bioanalytical applications. Although these semiconductor QDs have been accepted as ECL indicators for their effective and valuable ECL behavior, one challenge regarding these heavy metals have raised serious health and environmental concerns. Therefore, due to the low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, GQDs are demonstrated to be an eco-friendly material as well as excellent ECL labeling agents for biosensor [11].

Recently, electrochemical and luminescent methods alone as well as their combination have been widely employed as analytical methods on paper-based analytical devices, such as absorbance and fluorescence [12] chemiluminescence [13], electrochemistry [14], and electrochemiluminescence [15]. Efforts have been made to improve the immobilization of bio-recognition substance in paper, as well as the signal amplification for paper-based assays. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have attracted much attention in different bio-affinity assays due to their unique physical and chemical properties, such as easily controllable size distribution, long-term stability, superior conductivity, large surface area, and biocompatibility. Our groups have developed some paper-based devices based on a AuNPs modified porous paper working electrode, through the growth of an interconnected AuNPs layer on the surfaces of cellulose fibers, for point-of-care diagnosis [16,17]. In this work, an interconnected gold nanoflowers (AuNFs) layer was grown on the surfaces of cellulose fibers in the paper working zone fabricate a novel paper working electrode (PWE) on a paper-based device. In addition, for the first time, a novel strategy for ultrasensitive ECL immunosensor based on the green-luminescent N-GQDs oxidation using AuNFs modified paper as working electrode (Au-PWE) has been proposed. The N-GQDs were prepared by a facile solvothermal method using dimethylformamide (DMF) as a solvent and nitrogen source. With a sandwich-type immunoassay format, the system exhibited excellent ECL behaviors for the detection of AFP, high sensitivity, low detection limit and long-term stability. Such kind of good performance could be attributed to many reasons: (1) Employing carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) as signal carrier, a novel MWCNTs/N-GQDs ECL signal amplification labels were synthesized. (2) The 2-(dibutylamino)ethanol (DBAE) as coreactant was introduced into the solution, a great enhancement of ECL signal was observed. (3) A AuNFs modified porous morphology of paper working electrode, which can increase the surface area to capture a large amount of primary antibodies (Ab1) as well as improve the electronic transmission rate. The multiple amplification ECL strategy presented an important strategy to design ultrasensitive ECL sensors and expand their applications of ultratrace sensing.

All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade or the highest purity grade (commercially available) and used without further purification. Graphite powder, N-(3-dimethylamminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Thioglycolic acid (TGA) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). AFP (Ag), monoclonal antibody to AFP (Ab1 and Ab2), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were obtained from Shanghai Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd. (China). The aqueous solutions unless indicated were prepared with Milli-Q water (Millipore, USA) throughout. Phosphate buffer (PB) (10mM, pH 7.4) containing 0.05% (w/v) Tween-20 was used as washing buffer. PB (10mM, pH 7.4) containing 0.5% (w/v) BSA was used as blocking buffer. The clinical serum samples were provided by Shandong Tumor Hospital (China). Carbon ink (ED423ss) and Ag/AgCl ink (CNC-01) were purchased from Acheson. Whatman chromatography paper #1 (20.0cm×20.0cm) (pure cellulose paper) was obtained from GE Healthcare Worldwide (Pudong Shanghai, China) and used with further adjustment of size.

The ECL experiments were carried out on a MPI-B multipleparameter chemiluminescence analytical testing system equipped with an MPI-A/B multifunction chemiluminescence detector (Xi’An Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co. Ltd. Xi’an, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy Sciences, China) at room temperature. Cyclic voltammetric (CV) measurements were performed with a CHI 760D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments Inc., China). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on a CHI 604D Electrochemical Workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments Inc., China). UV–vis spectra were recorded on a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Bruker VECTOR22 Spectrometer using KBr pellets. Photoluminescence spectra were obtained on a RF-5301PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded using a JEOL-JSM-6300 scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from a Hitachi H-800 microscope (Japan).

The N-GQD was prepared through a facile one-pot solvothermal method [18]. In brief, the graphene oxide (GO), synthesized from natural graphite powder by an improved Hummers method [19], was dissolved in dimethylformamide (DMF) with a concentration of ∼200mgmL−1. The GO and DMF mixed solution was ultrasonicated for 30min and then transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave (30mL) and heated at 200°C for 4.5h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the obtained mixture was filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane to remove the black precipitates, obtaining a brown solution. The solvent was removed with the aid of a rotary evaporator. Then the obtained N-GQD was dispersed in Milli-Q water. The resulting solution was further dialyzed through a 500Da dialyzer for at least 48hours to totally remove the residues. Then the obtained pure N-GQD (final concentration, 0.15mgmL−1) was used for further characterization and kept in refrigerator at 4°C.

For the preparation of MWCNTs/GQDs composites, 5mL acid-treated MWCNTs (2mgmL−1) were first dispersed in a mixture of 1.0mL of the prepared N-GQDs, 1.0mL of EDC (100mgmL−1) and 1.0mL of NHS (100mgmL−1). The mixed suspension was stirred at 4°C for 12h. Unbound N-GQDs were removed by successive centrifugation and the suspension was washed with water for several times. Finally, the obtained MWCNTs/N-GQDs composites were dispersed in water with a final volume of 1.0mL for further use.

For the preparation of MWCNTs/N-GQDs/Ab2 labels, the obtained MWCNTs/N-GQDs composites was transferred into 2.0mL of 0.10M pH 7.4 PB containing 100mgmL−1 EDC and 100mgmL−1 NHS for 30min at 37°C. The mixed solution was then centrifuged and the resultant precipitates were redispersed in PB (10mM pH 7.4) containing 1.0mgmL−1 Ab2 for 12h at 37°C. An amount of 400μL of blocking buffer solution was then introduced in the mixed solution to block the nonspecific binding sites of the MWCNTs/N-GQDs/Ab2 labels. The final solution was then purified by centrifugation and the MWCNTs/N-GQDs/Ab2 labels were dispersed in PB (10mM pH 7.4) and stored at 4°C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the paper-based ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The paper-based device was fabricated on pure cellulose paper within 10min, and a detailed procedure was described in the supporting information. As shown in Scheme S1, on each wax patterned paper device (30mm×15mm), there were two circular paper working zones (named circle-a: 8.0mm in diameter and circle-b: 6.0mm in diameter) for screen-printing of electrodes (half-ring like counter electrode, reference electrode and working electrode). Prior to the immobilizing the corresponding capture antibodies, growth of AuNFs layer on the surfaces of cellulose fibers in the paper sample zone of PWE was implemented to fabricate porous Au-PWE with enhanced conductivity and enlarged effective surface area. Detailed procedures were described in the supporting information.

The fabrication procedure of the ECL immunosensor is shown in Scheme 1 . First, 20.0mM (20μL) TGA ethanol solution was dropped onto the prepared Au-PWE for 12h. The newly formed TGA/Au-PWE was rinsed with ethanol and water and dried with a stream of nitrogen. Subsequently, 20μL of freshly prepared EDC (100mgmL−1) and 20μL of NHS (100mgmL−1) were added and then incubated for 2h. After being washed thoroughly with pH 7.4 PB, Ab1 (20mgmL−1) was applied to the Au-PWE and reacted for 30min. Subsequently, excess antibodies were washed off with PB. Next, we blocked the Au-PWE by adding blocking buffer to block possible remaining active sites, and allowed Au-PWE to dry for 15min under ambient conditions. After another washing with buffer, the resulting paper-based ECL immunosensor was obtained and stored at 4°C in a dry environment prior to use.

The ECL assay procedures on the paper-based ECL immunosensor are shown in Scheme 1, and a detailed procedure is described below. Sample solutions (20μL) that contained different concentrations of AFP and MWCNTs/N-GQDs/Ab2 labels in PB were added to Au-PWE and allowed to incubate for 30min, respectively, followed by washing with washing buffer. For the ECL assay, the immunosensor was fixed and connected to the ECL system. Then, 40μL of PB solution (10mM, pH 7.4) containing 20.0mM DBAE was added, and the prepared ECL immunosensor was ready for AFP detection. CV measurements were used to trigger the ECL reaction in the sweeping range from 0 to 1.0V with a scan rate of 100mVs−1. The ECL emission was detected by the PMT biased at 800V. The ECL signals related to AFP concentrations could be detected.

The N-GQDs were prepared through a facile one-pot solvothermal approach using GO as a precursor. To effectively introduce nitrogen with strong electron-denoting effect into GQDs, DMF served as a solvent and source of nitrogen as it could be decomposed to dimethylamine [20]. Then the decomposed dimethylamine was doped into the GO to yield 1,2-amino alcohol via nucleophilic ring-opening reaction with the epoxy group on the surface of GO [21], thus enabling the extraction of smaller sp2 domains from the large GO sheet and simultaneous bonding of dimethylamine with the aromatic ring to form N-GQDs. The structural formula was illustrated in Fig. 1A.

A TEM image (Fig. S1) shows the uniformly dispersed N-GQDs with an average diameter of 3nm (Fig. S2). UV–vis spectrum of N-GQDs aqueous solution in Fig. 1B shows an absorption band at ca. 230nm, which is ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC. The optical absorption edge is found to be 390nm, revealing a 3.2eV optical bandgap of N-GQDs [18]. A fluorescence (PL) study is then carried out, as shown in Fig. 1C, the colorless N-GQDs solution emits bright green PL under irradiation with a 365nm UV lamp (inset in Fig. S1), the maximum excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength of the N-GQDs are 390 and 520nm, respectively. The obtained N-GQD is very stable, exhibiting a transparent homogeneous phase without any precipitation or agglomeration at room temperature for at least 20 months either in water or physiological conditions such as PB.

The surface chemistry of the N-GQDs was studied by FT-IR. FT-IR spectra of GO and N-GQD (Fig. 1D) confirms the nitrogen doping and partial reduction for N-GQD. Compared with the spectrum of GO, the epoxy band at 1073cm−1 completely disappears while two new bands at 1113 and 2924cm−1, which are respectively assigned to the CNC asymmetric stretching and the CH asymmetric stretching for the attached dimethylamido, can be observed for the spectrum of N-GQD. And the bands at 1376cm−1 (bending vibration of OH), 1630cm−1 (stretching vibrations CC), 1710cm−1 (stretching vibrations of CO in carbonyl groups), and 3433cm−1 (stretching vibrations of OH in hydroxyl) are present for both GO and N-GQD. These results suggest the elimination of epoxy groups and the chemical bonding of nitrogen groups on the basal CC plane, whereas most of the inactive carboxyl and hydroxyl groups are still retained at the edge of the N-GQD.

This Au-PWE was fabricated on pure cellulose paper. The hydrophilic electrochemical cells were fabricated by printing wax on the paper surface in a high-resolution printing mode. Owing to the 3D porous structure of paper, the melted wax can penetrate into the paper to decrease the hydrophilicity of paper remarkably. The contact angle on the front and back side wax-penetrated surface is 113° and 108° respectively [22]. After the curing process, the unprinted area maintains good hydrophilicity, flexibility and 3D porous structure and will not affect the further screen-printing of electrodes.

The PWE was modified through the growth of an AuNFs layer on the surfaces of cellulose fibers in paper sample zone from AuNPs seeds to fabricate the porous Au-PWE. The TEM image (Fig. 2A) of the as-prepared AuNPs seeds solution revealed a highly monodispersed AuNPs seeds suspension with an average diameter of ∼3.5nm. As shown in Fig. 2B, the porous paper sample zone, possessed high ratio of surface area to weight (9.5m2/g) with rough cellulose fibers [23], could offer an excellent adsorption microenvironment for the AuNPs seeds. As shown in Fig. 2C and D with the growth time increased (5 and 10min), the AuNPs seeds were rapidly enlarged by incubating in the growth solution under the self-catalytic reduction mechanism of AuNPs growth. Finally, a continuous and dense AuNFs conducting layer with interconnected AuNFs on the cellulose fiber surface in paper sample zone was observed, indicating that a good coverage of AuNFs on the surfaces of the cellulose fibers was obtained after 10min of growth.

The electrochemical properties of the resulted porous Au-PWE were investigated through CV toward 10.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.5M KCl. The bare PWE was also characterized under the same conditions for comparison. As shown in Fig. 3A, the bare PWE exhibited one set of well defined redox peaks toward [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−]. For the Au-PWE, there was a sharp increase of the peak current. The CV curves showed that the Au-PWE had a significant higher peak current and larger CV area compared with the bare one, indicating that the porous Au-PWE had a much larger effective surface area, which may be attributed to the high effective surface area of the AuNFs layer on cellulose fibers and the porous morphology of paper.

EIS is an effective method to monitor the changes of interfacial properties, allowing the understanding of chemical transformation and processes associated with the conductive electrode surface. The EIS results were in accordance with those of CV. As shown in Fig. 3B, when AuNFs were modified on the PWE surface, a much lower resistance was obtained, implying that AuNFs layer on cellulose fibers were excellent electric conducting material and accelerated the electron transfer.

The MWCNTs/N-GQDs/Ab2 was chosen as ECL label (characteristics of this label were summarized in the supporting information). DBAE was chosen as coreactant because it was high ECL efficiency, lower toxicity, greater solubility and less volatility than the other commonly coreactant such as tripropylamine [24]. Fig. 3C displays the ECL curves of the immunosensor in the absence (curve a) and presence (curve b) of coreactant DBAE by cycling the potential between 0 and 1.0V at 100mVs−1. A weak anodic ECL signal could be observed in the buffer solution without DBAE, however, the ECL intensity could be greatly enhanced by introducing DBAE into the buffer solution, indicating that coreactant DBAE may play a crucial role in the ECL process.

Furthermore, to investigate the possible ECL mechanism in this system, a series of experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. 3C, no ECL emission was recorded in 10mM, pH 7.4 PB containing 20.0mM DBAE, in the absence of N-GQDs (curve a), whereas the sandwich immune complex formed on the Au-PWE with ECL labels, displayed strong anodic ECL at about 0.84V (curve c). Consequently, analogous to anodic ECL pathways of other semiconductors and Ru complex [25] in the presence of coreactants, the possible electrochemical reactions of N-GQDs and DBAE on the electrode in the present work are summarized as follows: As the electrode potential is scanned to more positive, N-GQDs are oxidized to N-GQDs +. Concurrently, since DBAE is a kind of aliphatic tertiary amine, and its molecular structure is similar to tripropylamine (TPrA). Generally, upon oxidation, the short-lived DBAE radical cation is supposed to lose a proton from an α-carbon to form the strongly reducing intermediate DBAE. Since the reduction potential of N-GQDs + was less negative than that of DBAE, N-GQDs + could receive electrons from DBAE to populate the excited state N-GQDs*. While N-GQDs fall from excited state N-GQDs* to ground state N-GQDs, light is emitted. The possible ECL process was described as the following equations:
(1) N-GQDs→N-GQDs + +e−
(2) DBAE→DBAE + +e−
(3) DBAE + →DBAE +H+
(4) N-GQDs + +DBAE →N-GQDs*+DBAEfragments
(5) N-GQDs*→N-GQDs+hυ

As shown in Fig. 3C, in the presence of coreactant DBAE, there was one ECL emission peak at 0.84V with both N-GQDs/Ab2 (curve b) and MWCNTs/N-GQDs/Ab2 (curve c) labels. The ECL intensity of MWCNTs/N-GQDs/Ab2 is 6 times higher than that of N-GQDs/Ab2, indicating that MWCNTs is a good ECL signal amplifier. Some possible explanations might be considered as follows: (1) The MWCNTs could display a high surfaceto-volume ratio, which could enhance the immobilized amount of biomolecules. (2) The presence of MWCNTs decreased the potential barrier of the ECL reduction due to the accelerated electron transport between the electrode and N-GQDs. (3) MWCNTs could accelerate electron transfer between the electrode and DBAE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analytical performance of ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: A series of experimental parameters, including solution pH, incubation time, scan rate, are investigated in detail to acquire the optimal ECL performance, as depicted in supporting information. Under optimal conditions, the sandwich-typed immunoassay was applied for the detection of AFP. Fig. 4A depicts the ECL profiles for the immunosensor after reacting with different concentrations of AFP. As can be seen, the ECL signal increased with the increase of concentrations of AFP as the consequence of the efficient capture of the AFP by sandwich immunoassay. As shown in Fig. 4B, a linear relation between ECL intensity and the logarithm of AFP concentration was obtained from 5.0pgmL−1 to 100.0ngmL−1 (R =0.995) with the detection limit of 1.2pgmL−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which was much lower than that with Ru(bpy)3 2+ or the other semiconductors based ECL sensing method [26–28].

To clarify the high performances of the proposed immunosensor toward AFP detection, the response characteristics of the developed immunoassay compared to those of other electrochemical or optical AFP immunoassays. The results presented in Table 1 obviously shown that the different characteristics of the proposed strategy for AFP biosensing are better in some cases or comparable with the other immunoassays reported so far [29–32]. Compared with other sensors reported previously our proposed immunosensor exhibited a satisfactory detection limit (LOD) and linear range. Because the cutoff value of AFP in the human normal is about 10ngmL−1 [33], the electrochemical immunoassay can completely meet the requirement of clinical diagnostics. Although the system has not yet been optimized for maximum efficiency [34], importantly, the LOD of the developed immunoassay was over 2000-fold lower than that of commercially available AFP ELISA kits (1.0ngmL−1, Genway Biotech., Inc.) (www.genwaybio.com).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, stability of the ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the specific recognition of antigenic species for the proposed ECL immunoassay, the as-prepared immunosensor was incubated in the different interfering agents, such as carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), carcinoma antigen 125 (CA-125) and prostate protein antigen (PSA). The results were shown in Fig. 4C. It can be seen that significantly higher ECL intensity can be observed for the target AFP than other biomarkers. These results clearly indicate the excellent selectivity of the as-proposed AFP ECL immunosensor. Reproducibility of the immunosensor for AFP determination was estimated with seven immunosensors made at Au-PWE, and the ECL intensity of the immunosensors demonstrated a relative standard derivation of 3.2% with AFP at 1.0ngmL−1 level.

The anodic ECL stability was also investigated for the purpose of the potential application in the analytical chemistry field. Fig. 4D displayed the ECL intensities of immunosensor under 10 repeated cyclic voltammetric scans in the potential window of 0–1.0V (vs Ag/AgCl) in PB (10mM pH 7.4) containing 0.10M KCl and 20mM DBAE at 100mVs−1. The anodic ECL intensity from N-GQDs was quite stable with a relative standard deviation of 0.23%, indicating its satisfying reversibility and reliability as a sensing signal.

To further investigate the feasibility of the immunosensor for practical applications, the proposed method were evaluated by comparing the assay results of clinical serum samples (fresh clinical human serums from a local hospital) with the reference values obtained by commercial electrochemiluminescent single-analyte tests (ECL measurements were carried out on Elecsys 2010 Immunoassay Analyzers in Shandong Tumor Hospital). When the level of AFP was over the calibration range, the serum sample was appropriately diluted with 10mM pH 7.4 PB, prior to assaying. As listed in Table 2 , the concentration of AFP obtained with the proposed immunoassay was close to that obtained with the commercial ECL test (the relative errors were in the range of −3.03–2.39%). All these results indicated the acceptable accuracy of the proposed ECL immunoassay for the detection of AFP in clinical samples.

In conclusion, a novel ultrasensitive anodic ECL immunoassay was developed by using green-luminescent N-GQDs as ECL labels, DBAE as coreactant. Furthermore, as an amplified element, MWCNTs/N-GQDs composites were used to produce an amplified ECL signal and improve the sensitivity of the immunosensor. The model protein, AFP, was successfully immobilized into a novel porous Au-PWE, fabricated through the growth of the AuNFs layer on the surfaces of cellulose fibers in PWE, which increased the surface area, retained the biologic activity of the antibody, and provided a good pathway of electron transfer, further enhanced the sensitivity. The resulting immunodevice for quantitative detection of AFP possesses high sensitivity, good reproducibility, and long-term stability. We anticipate that this proposed method can be expanded readily for detecting other cancer biomarkers and has the potential for reliable early diagnosis of cancer and other diseases.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475052, 51273084, 21175058), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2012BZ002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.07.012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Transparent luminescent hyperbranched epoxy/carbon oxide dot nanocomposites with outstanding toughness and ductility
Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots
Facile synthetic method for pristine graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide quantum dots: origin of blue and green luminescence
Boron and nitrogen doping induced half-metallicity in zigzag triwing graphene nanoribbons
Polyelectrolyte functionalized carbon nanotubes as efficient metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction
Integrated tyramide and polymerization-assisted signal amplification for a highly-sensitive immunoassay
Supersensitive detection of chlorinated phenols by multiple amplification electrochemiluminescence sensing based on carbon quantum dots/graphene
Electrochemiluminescence ratiometry: a new approach to DNA biosensing
Electrochemiluminescence of blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots and its application in ultrasensitive aptasensor for adenosine triphosphate detection
Rapid and high sensitive detection of mercury ions using a fluorescence based paper test strip with a N-alkylaminopyrazole ligand as receptor
Paper-based chemiluminescence ELISA: lab-on-paper based on chitosan modified paper device and wax-screen-printing
Electrochemical detection in a paper-based separation device
Quantum dot (QD)-modified carbon tape electrodes for reproducible electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emission on a paper-based platform
Photoelectrochemical sensor for pentachlorophenol on microfluidic paper-based analytical device based on the molecular imprinting technique
A three-dimensional origami-based immuno-biofuel cell for self-powered, low-cost, and sensitive point-of-care testing
Strong two-photon-induced fluorescence from photostable, biocompatible nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for cellular and deep-tissue imaging
A novel strategy for making soluble reduced graphene oxide sheets cheaply by adopting an endogenous reducing agent
Optically tunable amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Three-dimensional paper-based electrochemiluminescence immunodevice for multiplexed measurement of biomarkers and point-of-care testing
Bioactive paper provides a low-cost platform for diagnostics
Quantum dot electrochemiluminescence in aqueous solution at lower potential and its sensing application
Quenching of the electrochemiluminescence of tris(2,2′-bipyridine)ruthenium(II)/tri-n-propylamine by pristine carbon nanotube and its application to quantitative detection of DNA
Microchip device with 64-site electrode array for multiplexed immunoassay of cell surface antigens based on electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer
Sandwich-type electrochemiluminescence immunosensor based on Ru-silica@Au composite nanoparticles labeled anti-AFP

The sandwich-type electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for α-fetoprotein based on enrichment by Fe3O4-Au magnetic nano probes and signal amplification by CdS-Au composite nanoparticles labeled anti-AFP
A label-free amperometric immunosensor for α-fetoprotein determination based on highly ordered porous multi-walled carbon nanotubes/silica nanoparticles
Simultaneous determination of carcinoembryonic antigen and α-fetoprotein using an ITO immunoelectrode modified with gold nanoparticles and mesoporous silica
Silver-graphene oxide nanocomposites as redox probes for electrochemical determination of α-fetoprotein
Electrodeposition of CdSe quantum dots and its application to an electrochemiluminescence immunoassay for α-fetoprotein
Additional molecular biological amplification strategy for enhanced sensitivity of monitoring low-abundance protein with dual nanotags
Fabrication of a simple and sensitive QDs-based electrochemiluminescence immunosensor using a nanostructured composite material for the detection of tumor markers α-fetoprotein

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lina Zhang works in School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan as a lecturer.
Long Li studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as undergraduate student.
Chao Ma studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as a student.

Shenguang Ge received his PhD degree in Chemistry and Chemical Engineering in 2013 from Shandong University, completed his master degree studies in University of Jinan in 2006. He joined University of Jinan, where currently he is an associate professor of chemistry. His research interests are in the area of biosensor and chemsensor.

Mei Yan received her BSc in applied chemistry from University of Jinan in 1999, and obtained her PhD in 2005 from Institute of chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences. She then joined University of Jinan, as a professor working on the synthesis and performance of advanced functional materials.
Cuirong Bian works in Qilu Hospital of Shandong University, Jinan 250012, PR China.
